disclaimer: MVP EDUCATIONAL SUBSET - NOT LEGAL ADVICE. This rule pack is a condensed, paraphrased representation of selected AUSTRAC AML/CTF obligations for real estate, built for a prototype. Rule IDs (e.g. RE-CDD-01) are internal references for grounding, not official citations. All obligations, dates and thresholds must be verified against the AML/CTF Act 2006, the AML/CTF Rules, and current AUSTRAC guidance before any real-world use.

rules.title: Designated services (real estate)
rules.text: From 1 July 2026, a real estate business provides a designated service when, in the course of carrying on a business, it acts as an agent for a seller or buyer in the sale or transfer of real estate, or acts as a buyer's agent. Providing a designated service makes the business a reporting entity with AML/CTF obligations.

rules.title: Enrolment and registration
rules.text: A reporting entity must enrol with AUSTRAC. Enrolment opened on 31 March 2026; an entity providing designated services on 1 July 2026 must enrol by 29 July 2026.

rules.title: AML/CTF program
rules.text: A reporting entity must adopt and maintain a written AML/CTF program: an ML/TF risk assessment plus risk-based policies, procedures, systems and controls, with board and senior-management oversight.

rules.title: Initial CDD - identify and verify the customer
rules.text: Before or when providing a designated service, identify the customer and verify their identity using reliable and independent documents or data. For an individual, collect and verify full name, date of birth, and residential address.

rules.title: Who the customer is
rules.text: The customer is the party to whom the designated service is provided: for a selling agent this is the vendor; for a buyer's agent this is the purchaser. Identify and verify that customer.

rules.title: Beneficial ownership
rules.text: Where the customer is a company, trust, partnership or SMSF, identify the beneficial owners - the individuals who ultimately own or control the customer (for example, 25% or more ownership, or control) - including a trust's trustees, settlor and beneficiaries, and take reasonable measures to verify them.

rules.title: Ownership and control structure
rules.text: Understand the ownership and control structure of a non-individual customer. Complex or opaque structures with no clear commercial rationale are higher risk and warrant closer scrutiny.

rules.title: Verification standard
rules.text: Verify identity from reliable and independent sources (original or certified documents, or electronic data verification). Record what was verified and how it was verified.

rules.title: Politically exposed persons
rules.text: Determine whether the customer or a beneficial owner is a politically exposed person (PEP). Foreign PEPs are high risk and require enhanced CDD and senior-management approval; domestic and international-organisation PEPs require a risk-based approach.

rules.title: Sanctions screening
rules.text: Screen customers and beneficial owners against the DFAT Consolidated List and UN sanctions. Providing a designated service to a sanctioned person is prohibited; a true sanctions match is unacceptable risk and must be escalated and reported.

rules.title: Ongoing customer due diligence
rules.text: Monitor the business relationship and keep KYC information current on a risk basis. Review the customer when circumstances change or when red flags arise.

rules.title: Enhanced CDD triggers
rules.text: Apply enhanced CDD where ML/TF risk is high - for example a foreign PEP, a sanctions nexus, a high-risk jurisdiction, unusual or complex transactions, unexplained source of funds or wealth, or where a suspicious matter report has been or will be submitted.

rules.title: Source of funds and wealth
rules.text: For higher-risk customers, take reasonable measures to establish the source of funds and source of wealth, and assess whether they are consistent with what is known about the customer.

rules.title: Reliance on third parties
rules.text: A reporting entity may rely on certain third parties to carry out elements of CDD but remains responsible for compliance; reliance must meet the conditions set out in the AML/CTF rules.

rules.title: Suspicious matter reports
rules.text: Submit a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC when you form a suspicion on reasonable grounds that a matter relates to money laundering, terrorism financing, proceeds of crime, or an offence. Submit within 3 business days of forming the suspicion (within 24 hours if it relates to terrorism financing).

rules.title: Suspicious indicators (real estate)
rules.text: Indicators include: attempts to overpay; third-party or unexplained funding; large or unusual cash components; reluctance or refusal to provide identity; use of opaque structures to obscure ownership; transactions inconsistent with the customer's profile; and rushed transactions with little regard to price.

rules.title: Threshold transaction reports
rules.text: Report physical-currency (cash) transactions of A$10,000 or more, or the foreign-currency equivalent, to AUSTRAC as a Threshold Transaction Report (TTR).

rules.title: Record-keeping
rules.text: Keep CDD records and transaction records for 7 years and make them available to AUSTRAC on request.

rules.title: Tipping-off
rules.text: Do not disclose to the customer or any other person that a suspicious matter report has been or will be submitted, or that related information has been provided to AUSTRAC.
